title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
text:
Heart rate variability is widely used to probe autonomic cardiovascular regulation, yet existing models are either descriptive spectral summaries or mechanistic point-process models whose time-varying parameters lack explicit stochastic dynamics. We introduce the SDE-Inverse Gaussian (SDE-IG) framework, which couples two Ornstein-Uhlenbeck processes representing parasympathetic and sympathetic drives to an Inverse Gaussian first-passage observation model for inter-beat intervals. All eight free parameters — the decay rates \(a_p, a_s\), coupling magnitudes \(a_{ps}, a_{sp}\), diffusion amplitudes \(\sigma_p, \sigma_s\), and observation parameters \(\mu_0, \rho\), are locally identifiable from a single RR interval stream (the input sensitivities \(c_p, c_s\) require a non-trivial exogenous input and are not estimated in the single-stream protocol), the Fisher information matrix is block-diagonal and positive definite, closed-form and profile maximum likelihood estimators exist for all parameters, and a Monte Carlo recovery study (\(N = 250\) replications) confirms sub-percent relative bias and nominal 95% confidence interval coverage. A spike-to-spike Unscented Kalman Filter recovers the net autonomic drive \(\hat\Delta(t)\) and the individual branch estimates \([\hat p(t),\, \hat s(t)]\) in real time; once parameters are estimated, forward simulation of the fitted OU model generates individual branch trajectories consistent with the identified spectral structure. The architecture extends naturally to multi-stream data, where an additional physiological observation (respiratory, blood pressure) further constrains the common-mode direction, reducing branch-level posterior uncertainty. This framework provides a principled, estimable, and interpretable generative model for beat-to-beat autonomic dynamics.








Figure 1: Generative architecture of the SDE-IG model. Two coupled Ornstein-Uhlenbeck processes represent the parasympathetic (\(p(t)\), fast, HF) and sympathetic (\(s(t)\), slow, LF) autonomic drives. Their difference \(\Delta(t) = s(t) - p(t)\) enters the log link to produce the time-varying mean interval \(\mu(t) = \mu_0 \exp(-\Delta(t))\). At each moment, the elapsed time since the last beat serves as a running clock for an Inverse Gaussian first-passage hazard; a beat fires when the clock crosses a stochastic threshold. The resulting spike train is the sole observable; all ten free parameters are estimated from it via a spike-to-spike UKF (inference, right panel).

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
text:
3.1 Latent Autonomic Dynamics
The RR interval series constrains the latent autonomic state through a single scalar observable: the net autonomic drive
\[
\Delta(t) \;=\; s(t) \;-\; p(t),
\]
which enters the mean interval via the log link. The beat-to-beat likelihood is therefore most directly informative about \(\Delta(t)\), and the filter posterior contracts tightest along the differential direction \(\mathbf{e}_{-} = [-1,\,1]^\top/\!\sqrt{2}\). However, this does not mean the individual branches are fundamentally unrecoverable. The OU parameters \(\{a_p,\,a_s,\,\sigma_p,\,\sigma_s\}\) — estimated from the same RR series — fully characterize the spectral structure of each branch: \(p(t)\) contributes power near \(a_p/(2\pi)\) Hz (the vagal, HF pole) and \(s(t)\) near \(a_s/(2\pi)\) Hz (the sympathetic, LF pole). Because \(a_p \gg a_s\), these poles are well-separated and individually identified from the spectral curvature of \(\Delta(t)\). Two reconstruction pathways are therefore available once parameters are estimated: (1) The UKF maintains a full 2D posterior \([\hat p_k,\,\hat s_k]\) with per-branch posterior SDs; the common-mode uncertainty \(\sigma_{p+s}\) is larger than the differential uncertainty \(\sigma_{p-s}\) but remains bounded by the OU stationary variance. (2) Forward simulation of the fitted OU model with \(\hat\Theta\) generates individual branch trajectories that are statistically consistent with the identified spectral structure and the observed \(\hat\Delta(t)\).
The additive-constant non-identifiability (any constant added to both \(p\) and \(s\) cancels in \(\Delta\)) is handled by the OU prior: both branches are mean-reverting to zero, so the common mode is bounded by \(\sigma_p^2/(2a_p) + \sigma_s^2/(2a_s)\). The result is that all dynamical parameters of both branches — decay rates, noise amplitudes, and coupling magnitudes — are recoverable from the single RR stream.
An additional identifiability constraint arises from the observation gains. The original formulation contained free sensitivity coefficients \(k_\text{par}\) and \(k_\text{sym}\) that scaled the contribution of each branch to the mean interval. Because the data identifies only \(\Delta(t)\), the gains and the diffusion amplitudes \(\sigma_p\), \(\sigma_s\) share a common scale: multiplying \(k_\text{sym}\) by \(\alpha\) and dividing \(s(t)\) and \(\sigma_s\) by \(\alpha\) leaves the likelihood unchanged for any \(\alpha > 0\). This scale aliasing is resolved by normalizing \(k_\text{par} = k_\text{sym} = 1\), absorbing the effective scale into \(\sigma_p\) and \(\sigma_s\). These are then interpretable as the amplitudes of autonomic noise in units directly linked to the log mean interval.
The resulting net drive \(\Delta(t) = s(t) - p(t)\) is, under the constraints imposed here (Section 3.2), a linear combination of two independent Ornstein-Uhlenbeck processes. Its spectral density function has two poles: one in the low-frequency (LF) band determined by \(a_s\), and one in the high-frequency (HF) band determined by \(a_p\). This is precisely the continuous-time ARMA(2,1) [CARMA(2,1)] spectral structure that a single RR stream can identify. Its parameters are the LF and HF power amplitudes, corresponding to \(\sigma_s^2/(2a_s)\) and \(\sigma_p^2/(2a_p)\) respectively, together with the baseline mean interval \(\mu_0\) and the shape parameter \(\kappa\).

3.1.1 Structural and Free Parameter Decomposition
The system matrix is
\[
A = \begin{pmatrix} -a_p & -a_{ps} \\ -a_{sp} & -a_s \end{pmatrix}
\]
with four non-negative rate parameters. The self-decay rates \(a_p\) and \(a_s\) govern the intrinsic time constants of each branch (\(\tau_p = 1/a_p\), \(\tau_s = 1/a_s\)); the cross-inhibition rates \(a_{ps}\) and \(a_{sp}\) encode accentuated antagonism, the well-documented mechanism by which sympathetic activation actively withdraws vagal tone and vice versa. All four parameters are estimated from the RR interval series; neuroscientific literature provides physiologically plausible ranges that serve as prior information for starting values (\(a_p \approx 2\) Hz, \(a_s \approx 0.2\) Hz) but not as fixed constraints.
Linear stability requires \(\det(A) = a_p a_s - a_{ps} a_{sp} > 0\), which is enforced as a hard constraint during estimation. When the antagonism terms are zero (\(a_{ps} = a_{sp} = 0\)), the system reduces to two independent Ornstein-Uhlenbeck processes. Identifiability of the input sensitivities \(c_p\) and \(c_s\) additionally requires a non-trivial exogenous input \(u(t) \not\equiv 0\). The coupling magnitudes \(a_{ps}\) and \(a_{sp}\) are identified from the second-order spectral structure of \(\Delta(t)\): non-zero coupling shifts both eigenvalues of \(A\) away from \(\{-a_p, -a_s\}\), producing a detectable change in the spectral peak positions. Their separate identification from the self-decay rates requires sufficient spectral resolution, which scales with recording duration (see Section 4.7).
The parameter set is therefore partitioned as follows.

3.1.2 The Net Autonomic Drive
The quantity directly estimated from the RR series is \(\Delta(t) = s(t) - p(t)\). In the baseline uncoupled case (\(a_{ps} = a_{sp} = 0\)),
\[
d\Delta(t) \;=\; ds(t) - dp(t) \;=\;
\bigl[-a_s s(t) + c_s u(t)\bigr]dt + \sigma_s\,dW_s
-\bigl[-a_p p(t) + c_p u(t)\bigr]dt - \sigma_p\,dW_p
\]
Because \(a_p \neq a_s\), \(\Delta(t)\) is not itself an OU process but a difference of two OU processes with distinct time constants. When coupling is nonzero, the drift of \(\Delta(t)\) involves a linear combination of both \(p(t)\) and \(s(t)\) individually Section 4.5, and \(\Delta(t)\) alone is no longer sufficient to summarize the full state; the two-dimensional filter of Section 5 is required.
The spectral density of \(\Delta(t)\) is determined by the eigenvalues of \(A\). Letting \(\gamma_{1,2} = \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[-(a_p + a_s) \mp \sqrt{(a_p - a_s)^2 + 4 a_{ps} a_{sp}}\bigr]\) (both negative when the stability condition holds), the spectral density takes the form of two Lorentzians centred at \(|\gamma_1|/(2\pi)\) and \(|\gamma_2|/(2\pi)\):
\[
S_\Delta(f) \;\approx\; \frac{A_1}{\gamma_1^2 + (2\pi f)^2}
                      + \frac{A_2}{\gamma_2^2 + (2\pi f)^2}
\]
where \(A_1, A_2 > 0\) are amplitude coefficients that depend on all four rate parameters and the diffusion amplitudes. When \(a_{ps} = a_{sp} = 0\), this collapses to the familiar two-Lorentzian form with poles at \(a_s/(2\pi)\) and \(a_p/(2\pi)\). Non-zero antagonism spreads the poles: \(|\gamma_1|\) increases above \(\max(a_p, a_s)\) and \(|\gamma_2|\) decreases below \(\min(a_p, a_s)\), producing a distinctive spectral signature from which all four rate parameters can, in principle, be identified. This spectrum is identifiable from the second-order properties of the RR interval series: the positions of the two spectral poles identify \(a_p\) and \(a_s\); the amplitudes of the two Lorentzians (proportional to \(\sigma_p^2/a_p\) and \(\sigma_s^2/a_s\) in the uncoupled limit; functions of all four rate parameters and both diffusion amplitudes when coupling is nonzero) encode the effective spectral power of each branch; and the non-zero coupling magnitudes \(a_{ps}\) and \(a_{sp}\) shift both poles away from \(a_p\) and \(a_s\), generating a distinctive spectral signature that distinguishes the coupled model from two independent OU processes. This directly links all ten free parameters to the spectral structure of the RR tachogram.

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
text:
3.2 The Constrained Stochastic Differential Equations
The autonomic state vector \(\mathbf{x}(t) = [p(t),\, s(t)]^\top\) evolves according to a system of coupled linear SDEs. The coupling terms \(a_{ps}\) and \(a_{sp}\) are free parameters estimated from data alongside \(a_p\), \(a_s\), \(\sigma_p\), \(\sigma_s\), \(\mu_0\), and \(\rho\). For the baseline single-stream protocol with no exogenous input, the data provide sufficient curvature to estimate the coupling magnitudes conditioned on the observed state trajectory Section 4.5; the uncoupled constraint \(a_{ps} = a_{sp} = 0\) is a special case used for comparison and for the initial simulation demonstrations. When coupling is nonzero, the OU log-likelihood blocks are no longer separable, and joint numerical optimization replaces the closed-form estimates Section 4. The full system is:
\[
\begin{aligned}
dp(t) &= \bigl[-a_p\, p(t) - a_{ps}\, s(t) + c_p\, u(t)\bigr]\,dt + \sigma_p\,dW_p(t) \\
ds(t) &= \bigl[-a_{sp}\, p(t) - a_s\, s(t) + c_s\, u(t)\bigr]\,dt + \sigma_s\,dW_s(t)
\end{aligned}
\]
where \(W_p\) and \(W_s\) are independent standard Wiener processes. Each branch is an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process: mean-reverting to the homeostatic baseline (or to the input-driven equilibrium when \(u(t) \neq 0\)), with a time constant and noise amplitude set by its respective parameters. The free parameter set is \(\{a_p,\, a_s,\, a_{ps},\, a_{sp},\, \sigma_p,\, \sigma_s,\, \mu_0,\, \rho\}\) (plus \(c_p, c_s\) when input is present); all are estimated from the RR interval series. The values \(a_p \approx 2\,\text{Hz}\) and \(a_s \approx 0.2\,\text{Hz}\) from the autonomic neuroscience literature serve only as starting values for the optimizer; no physiological prior constrains the estimates Section 4. The coupling terms \(a_{ps}\) and \(a_{sp}\) are always free parameters; their identifiability from the RR series is established in Section 4.5. When \(a_{ps} = a_{sp} = 0\) the system reduces to two independent OU processes — a constrained sub-model used in the initial demonstrations because it admits closed-form estimators and a block-diagonal FIM that simplify exposition. When a non-trivial external input is present, \(c_p\) and \(c_s\) are additionally identifiable, bringing the full active set to ten parameters.

3.2.1 Vagal Kinetics and the HF Band
The parasympathetic state \(p(t)\) acts as a high-pass filter on the Wiener process noise, with its spectral pole located at \(a_p/(2\pi)\) Hz. The self-decay rate \(a_p\) is a free parameter estimated from the RR series; values near \(2\) Hz (time constant \(\tau_p \approx 0.5\) s) are consistent with vagal physiology and are recovered in the simulation study below. The impulse response at these values decays within approximately \(2\)–\(3\) s, enabling beat-to-beat vagal modulation and generating spectral power in the HF band (\(0.15\)–\(0.4\) Hz) associated with respiratory sinus arrhythmia (Malik 1996). The correspondence between the estimated \(\hat a_p\) and the known physiological range provides an external validity check on the parameter recovery.


Code
t_imp <- seq(0, 40, by = 0.05)
imp_p <- ifelse(t_imp >= 1, exp(-PARAMS$free$a_p * (t_imp - 1)), 0)
imp_s <- ifelse(t_imp >= 1, exp(-PARAMS$free$a_s * (t_imp - 1)), 0)

par(mar = c(4, 4.5, 1.5, 1))
plot(t_imp, imp_p, type = "l", col = "#0072B2", lwd = 2.5,
     xlab = "Time (s)", ylab = "Response magnitude",
     frame.plot = FALSE, ylim = c(0, 1.05))
lines(t_imp, imp_s, col = "#D55E00", lwd = 2.5)
abline(v = 1, lty = 2, col = "gray60")
tau_p_val <- round(1 / PARAMS$free$a_p, 2)
tau_s_val <- round(1 / PARAMS$free$a_s, 1)
legend("topright",
       legend = c(
         bquote("Parasympathetic" ~~ (a[p] == .(PARAMS$free$a_p) ~ Hz ~
                                      "," ~ tau[p] == .(tau_p_val) ~ s)),
         bquote("Sympathetic"     ~~ (a[s] == .(PARAMS$free$a_s) ~ Hz ~
                                      "," ~ tau[s] == .(tau_s_val) ~ s))
       ), col = c("#0072B2", "#D55E00"), lwd = 2.5, bty = "n", cex = 0.85)
grid(col = "lightgray", lty = 1)








Figure 2: Theoretical impulse response of the vagal (blue) and sympathetic (red) branches. A unit impulse at \(t = 1\) s illustrates the order-of-magnitude difference in recovery time. The vagal response dissipates within seconds; the sympathetic response persists for tens of seconds.







3.2.2 Sympathetic Inertia and the LF Band
The sympathetic state \(s(t)\) acts as a low-pass filter, with its spectral pole at \(a_s/(2\pi)\) Hz. The self-decay rate \(a_s\) is estimated from data; values near \(0.2\) Hz (time constant \(\tau_s \approx 5\) s) are consistent with noradrenergic kinetics and recovered in the simulation study. Its slow decay integrates inputs over a prolonged window, producing the smooth, trending components of autonomic regulation. The emergent spectral power is concentrated in the LF band (\(0.04\)–\(0.15\) Hz) without any explicit oscillatory forcing, arising purely from the interaction of OU dynamics and the Wiener process.

section: 3.3 Visualization of the Latent State Trajectories
text:
3.3 Visualization of the Latent State Trajectories
Figure Figure 3 shows simulated trajectories under baseline conditions, illustrating the distinct spectral signatures of the two branches and the identifiable net drive \(\Delta(t) = s(t) - p(t)\).


Code
set.seed(101)
r0 <- sim_sde_ig(300, 0.005, PARAMS)

layout(matrix(1:3, 3, 1))
par(mar = c(1.5, 5, 1, 1.5), oma = c(3, 0, 0, 0))

plot(r0$time, r0$p, type = "l", col = "#0072B2", lwd = 1.2,
     ylab = "p(t)", xaxt = "n", frame.plot = FALSE)
abline(h = 0, lty = 2, col = "gray70"); 

plot(r0$time, r0$s, type = "l", col = "#D55E00", lwd = 2,
     ylab = "s(t)", xaxt = "n", frame.plot = FALSE)
abline(h = 0, lty = 2, col = "gray70"); 

plot(r0$time, r0$delta, type = "l", col = "#2C5F2E", lwd = 1.8,
     ylab = expression(Delta(t) == s(t) - p(t)),
     xlab = "Time (s)", frame.plot = FALSE)
abline(h = 0, lty = 2, col = "gray70"); 
mtext("Time (s)", side = 1, outer = TRUE, line = 1.5)








Figure 3: Simulated trajectories of the latent autonomic states under baseline conditions (\(u(t) = 0\), all free parameters at their reference values). Top: Parasympathetic state \(p(t)\), high-frequency texture driven by the fast decay rate. Middle: Sympathetic state \(s(t)\), smooth, slow-trending trajectory. Bottom: Net autonomic drive \(\Delta(t) = s(t) - p(t)\), the quantity directly identified by the RR interval series. Positive values indicate sympathetic dominance; negative values indicate vagal dominance.

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
text:
3.4 The Inverse Gaussian Observation Model

3.4.1 Biophysical Rationale: The Integrate-and-Fire Mechanism
The mapping from continuous latent autonomic states to discrete heartbeat times is grounded in the biophysics of the sinoatrial node. During diastole, the pacemaker membrane potential rises from a hyperpolarized resting level under the influence of a net inward depolarizing current. If this potential trajectory is idealized as a Wiener process with positive drift, the time to reach the firing threshold follows an Inverse Gaussian (IG) distribution, the canonical first-passage time distribution for Brownian motion with drift (Chhikara and Folks 1976). This provides a direct structural link between statistical modeling of inter-beat intervals and cardiac cellular electrophysiology, distinguishing the present framework from Poisson or Gaussian observation models that lack this mechanistic grounding.


3.4.2 The Log-Link Mean Interval
The time-varying mean interval \(\mu(t)\) connects the latent autonomic states to the location parameter of the IG distribution. We define this link as
\[
\mu(t) \;=\; \mu_0 \cdot \exp\!\bigl(p(t) - s(t)\bigr) \;=\; \mu_0 \cdot \exp\!\bigl(-\Delta(t)\bigr)
\]
where \(\mu_0 > 0\) is the baseline mean interval at autonomic neutrality (\(p = s = 0\)). This log link replaces the linear formulation used in earlier implementations and offers three essential properties.
First, \(\mu(t) > 0\) for all state values without exception, by construction. No clipping, flooring, or soft constraint is required. The linear link \(\mu(t) = 1/(\rho_0 + k_\text{sym}s - k_\text{par}p)\) can produce negative or undefined values when autonomic state fluctuations push the rate below zero, requiring ad hoc correction that biases the likelihood.
Second, the log link is the canonical link for positive continuous outcomes and encodes the physiologically appropriate multiplicative structure of autonomic modulation. Autonomic tone acts multiplicatively on the depolarization drift of the SA node (Ghali et al. 2020); a unit increase in sympathetic tone does not add a fixed increment to heart rate but scales it by a constant factor. The log link captures this precisely: \(\mu(t) = \mu_0 e^{-\Delta(t)}\), so each unit of \(\Delta(t)\) scales the mean interval by \(e^{-1} \approx 0.37\).
Third, the log link renders \(\log\mu(t) = \log\mu_0 - \Delta(t)\) a linear function of the latent state. This is the observation equation for a Gaussian state-space model, which simplifies the linearization step in the extended Kalman filter (Phase 4) and makes the connection to existing state-space HRV methods transparent.


Code
set.seed(123)
tt <- seq(0, 20, by = 0.02)
# Simulate a net drive with large excursions to stress-test positivity
del <- cumsum(rnorm(length(tt), 0, 0.06))
del <- del - mean(del)

rho0  <- 1 / 1.0   # baseline rate ~60 BPM
mu_ln <- 1.0 * exp(-del)
mu_li <- ifelse((rho0 + del) > 0.1, 1 / (rho0 + del), NA)

par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4.5, 4.5, 2.5, 1))

# Left: linear link, show where it fails
ylm <- c(-0.5, max(mu_li, na.rm = TRUE) * 1.1)
plot(tt, mu_li, type = "l", col = "#D55E00", lwd = 2,
     xlab = "Time (s)", ylab = expression(mu(t) ~ "[s]"),
     main = "Linear Link", frame.plot = FALSE, ylim = ylm)
rect(xleft = 0, xright = max(tt), ybottom = -0.6, ytop = 0,
     col = rgb(0.8, 0, 0, 0.12), border = NA)
abline(h = 0, lty = 2, col = "gray50")
text(10, -0.25, "Invalid domain\n(clipping required)",
     col = "firebrick", cex = 0.8, adj = 0.5)

# Right: log link, always positive
plot(tt, mu_ln, type = "l", col = "#0072B2", lwd = 2,
     xlab = "Time (s)", ylab = expression(mu(t) ~ "[s]"),
     main = "Log Link (this model)", frame.plot = FALSE)
abline(h = 1.0, lty = 2, col = "gray50")
text(1, 0.8, expression(mu[0] == 1.0 ~ s), adj = 0, col = "gray40", cex = 0.8)








Figure 4: Comparison of the log link and the linear link for a simulated net drive \(\Delta(t) = s(t) - p(t)\). Left: The linear link \(\mu(t) = 1/(\rho_0 + \Delta(t))\) becomes undefined and negative when \(\Delta(t)\) crosses below \(-\rho_0\), requiring clipping. Right: The log link \(\mu(t) = \mu_0 \exp(-\Delta(t))\) is always positive and smooth. The shaded region on the left marks the invalid domain.







3.4.3 The Probability Density Function
Given that the previous beat occurred at time \(u_k\), the density of the next beat time \(t > u_k\) is
\[
f(t \mid u_k,\, \mathbf{x}(t)) \;=\;
\left[\frac{\kappa}{2\pi(t - u_k)^3}\right]^{1/2}
\exp\!\left\{
  -\frac{\kappa\bigl[(t-u_k) - \mu(t)\bigr]^2}{2\,\mu(t)^2\,(t-u_k)}
\right\},
\]
where \(\tau = t - u_k\) is the elapsed time since the last beat and \(\kappa = \mu_0/\rho^2 > 0\) is the derived shape parameter and \(\rho = \sqrt{\mu_0/\kappa}\) is the baseline coefficient of variation estimated directly from the data. In the first-passage interpretation, \(\rho\) is proportional to the threshold-to-noise ratio of the SA node membrane: small \(\rho\) corresponds to a near-deterministic rhythm; large \(\rho\) to a highly variable, noise-dominated one. Typical resting values are \(\rho \approx 0.15\)–\(0.30\).
The shape parameter \(\kappa > 0\) controls interval regularity but is not directly interpretable in variance units. We therefore work with the baseline coefficient of variation
\[
\rho \;=\; \sqrt{\mu_0/\kappa}
\]
so that \(\kappa = \mu_0/\rho^2\) and \(\operatorname{Var}(\tau_0) = \rho^2 \mu_0^2\) at autonomic neutrality (\(\Delta = 0\)). For a general state, \(\operatorname{Var}(\tau) = \rho^2 \mu(t)^3/\mu_0\), preserving the heteroscedastic property while making the baseline variability directly readable: \(\rho \approx 0.22\) corresponds to a coefficient of variation of 22%, typical of resting HRV. The free parameter estimated from data is \(\rho\); all likelihood computations use \(\kappa = \mu_0/\rho^2\) internally.
A distinctive property of the IG distribution is its heteroscedastic variance: \(\text{Var}(\tau) = \mu(t)^3/\kappa\). The cubic dependence on the mean implies that as heart rate increases (and \(\mu\) decreases), beat-to-beat variability decreases automatically, even when \(\kappa\) is held constant. This provides a mechanistic account of the empirically well-documented reduction in HRV during exercise and stress, without requiring any model-specific adjustments.


3.4.4 Log-Space Hazard Computation
For the conditional intensity function, the instantaneous probability of a beat at time \(t\) given survival to time \(t\), we require the hazard rate \(\lambda(t \mid \mathcal{H}_t) = f(\tau)/S(\tau)\), where \(S(\tau) = 1 - F(\tau)\) is the survival function. The IG CDF is
\[
F(\tau;\mu,\kappa) \;=\;
\Phi\!\left(\sqrt{\tfrac{\kappa}{\tau}}\Bigl(\tfrac{\tau}{\mu}-1\Bigr)\right)
+
\exp\!\left(\tfrac{2\kappa}{\mu}\right)
\Phi\!\left(-\sqrt{\tfrac{\kappa}{\tau}}\Bigl(\tfrac{\tau}{\mu}+1\Bigr)\right)
\]
The term \(\exp(2\kappa/\mu)\) grows rapidly: at \(\kappa = 15\), \(\mu = 0.5\) s, its value is \(e^{60} \approx 10^{26}\). Although this is within IEEE 754 double-precision range, the numerical product \(\exp(2\kappa/\mu) \cdot \Phi(-z_2)\) involves multiplying a huge number by a tiny one, causing catastrophic cancellation that propagates into both the survival function and the hazard. More critically, any gradient-based optimization of the likelihood will encounter numerical instability at precisely the parameter values of greatest interest, those where heart rate is elevated.
The resolution is to compute the log-survival entirely in log space. Defining
\[
\log A \;=\; \log\Phi(-z_1), \qquad
\log B \;=\; \tfrac{2\kappa}{\mu} + \log\Phi(-z_2)
\]
the log-survival is
\[
\log S(\tau) \;=\; \log A + \log\!\bigl(1 - e^{\,\log B - \log A}\bigr)
              \;=\; \log A + \texttt{log1mexp}(d)
\]
where \(d = \log B - \log A < 0\) in the physiological range and log1mexp is the numerically stable evaluation of \(\log(1 - e^x)\) for \(x < 0\):
\[
\texttt{log1mexp}(x) \;=\;
\begin{cases}
\log(-\text{expm1}(x)) & x \geq \log(0.5) \\
\text{log1p}(-\exp(x)) & x < \log(0.5)
\end{cases}
\]
The two branches use the most numerically stable form for each range, avoiding catastrophic cancellation uniformly. The log-hazard is then simply \(\log\lambda(\tau) = \log f(\tau) - \log S(\tau)\), computed entirely in log space with no intermediate overflow.


Code
t_sq  <- seq(0.01, 2.2, length.out = 400)
dig_v <- function(t, mu, k) exp(log_ig_pdf(t, mu, k))

mu_v  <- c(0.5, 0.85, 1.2)
k_v   <- c(5, 15, 50)
cols  <- c("#D55E00", "black", "#0072B2")

par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4.5, 4.5, 2.5, 1))

# Left: varying mu
plot(NA, xlim = c(0, 2.2), ylim = c(0, 6),
     xlab = "Interval (s)", ylab = "Density",
     main = "Effect of mean interval \u03bc", frame.plot = FALSE)
for (i in 1:3) lines(t_sq, dig_v(t_sq, mu_v[i], 15), col = cols[i], lwd = 2)
legend("topright",
       legend = paste("\u03bc =", mu_v, "s"),
       col = cols, lwd = 2, bty = "n", cex = 0.85)

# Right: varying kappa
plot(NA, xlim = c(0, 2.2), ylim = c(0, 6),
     xlab = "Interval (s)", ylab = "Density",
     main = "Effect of shape \u03ba", frame.plot = FALSE)
for (i in 1:3) lines(t_sq, dig_v(t_sq, 0.85, k_v[i]), col = cols[i], lwd = 2)
legend("topright",
       legend = paste("\u03ba =", k_v),
       col = cols, lwd = 2, bty = "n", cex = 0.85)








Figure 5: The Inverse Gaussian probability density. Left: Effect of the mean interval \(\\mu\) (shape fixed at \(\\kappa = 15\)). As \(\\mu\) decreases (sympathetic dominance), the distribution shifts to earlier times. Right: Effect of the shape parameter \(\\kappa\) (mean fixed at \(\\mu = 1.00\) s). Higher \(\\kappa\) corresponds to lower variance, a more regular rhythm. The heteroscedastic property \(\\text{Var}(\\tau) = \\mu^3/\\kappa\) links both panels: reducing \(\\mu\) reduces the variance even at fixed \(\\kappa\). These are illustrative parameter values chosen to show the shape of the distribution across a physiological range; the reference simulation uses \(\\mu_0 = `r PARAMS\)free\(mu_0`\) s and \(\\kappa = `r round(kappa_from_rho(PARAMS\)free\(mu_0, PARAMS\)free\(rho))`\).







3.4.5 The Hazard Function and Its Physiological Significance
The resulting hazard function \(\lambda(\tau)\) is non-monotonic. At \(\tau = 0\), the hazard is zero, correctly modeling the absolute refractory period of cardiac tissue. It rises steeply as \(\tau\) approaches \(\mu(t)\), the membrane potential nears threshold, then peaks and gradually decreases in the tail. Under the log link, a shift in \(\Delta(t)\) compresses or stretches this profile continuously, modulating the probability of an earlier or later beat without discontinuity.


Code
tau_v  <- seq(0.01, 2.5, length.out = 500)

kap_fig <- kappa_from_rho(PARAMS$free$mu_0, PARAMS$free$rho)
mu_base <- PARAMS$free$mu_0

h_base  <- vapply(tau_v, ig_hazard, numeric(1), mu = mu_base,        kappa = kap_fig)
h_sym   <- vapply(tau_v, ig_hazard, numeric(1), mu = mu_base * 0.60, kappa = kap_fig)
h_para  <- vapply(tau_v, ig_hazard, numeric(1), mu = mu_base * 1.40, kappa = kap_fig)

mu_ax  <- seq(mu_base * 0.4, mu_base * 1.5, length.out = 60)
tau_ax <- seq(0.02, 1.8, length.out = 60)
hgrid  <- outer(tau_ax, mu_ax, Vectorize(function(ta, mu) ig_hazard(ta, mu, kap_fig)))

par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4.5, 5, 2.5, 1))

plot(tau_v, h_base, type = "l", lwd = 2.5, col = "black",
     ylim = c(0, max(c(h_base, h_sym, h_para)) * 1.08),
     xlab = "Time since last beat (s)",
     ylab = expression(lambda(tau ~ "|" ~ H[t])),
     main = "Hazard Function", frame.plot = FALSE)
lines(tau_v, h_sym,  lwd = 2.5, col = "#D55E00", lty = 2)
lines(tau_v, h_para, lwd = 2.5, col = "#0072B2", lty = 3)
legend("topright",
       legend = c(bquote(mu == .(mu_base) ~ s ~ "(baseline)"),
                  bquote(mu == .(round(mu_base * 0.60, 2)) ~ s ~ "(sympathetic \u2191)"),
                  bquote(mu == .(round(mu_base * 1.40, 2)) ~ s ~ "(vagal \u2191)")),
       col = c("black", "#D55E00", "#0072B2"),
       lwd = 2.5, lty = c(1, 2, 3), bty = "n", cex = 0.85)

image(tau_ax, mu_ax, hgrid,
      col  = hcl.colors(100, "YlOrRd", rev = TRUE),
      xlab = "Time since last beat (s)",
      ylab = expression("Mean interval" ~ mu(t) ~ "[s]"),
      main = "Hazard Surface", axes = FALSE)
axis(1); axis(2)
contour(tau_ax, mu_ax, hgrid, add = TRUE,
        col = "black", nlevels = 5, lwd = 0.8)








Figure 6: The Inverse Gaussian hazard function \(\lambda(\tau \mid \mathcal{H}_t)\), computed using the fully log-space algorithm. Left: Effect of sympathetic activation: reducing \(\mu(t)\) shifts the hazard peak to the left, increasing the probability of an earlier beat. Right: The hazard surface as a function of both elapsed time and the current mean interval. Brighter colors indicate higher instantaneous firing probability.

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
text:
3.5 Numerical Simulation Algorithm
The computational implementation of the framework requires a hybrid scheme that bridges continuous-time latent dynamics and discrete event generation. Three structural improvements over naive implementations are incorporated: an exact discretization of the OU transition, a fully log-space intensity evaluation, and a survival-ratio event generation procedure that avoids the systematic bias of the Bernoulli approximation.

3.5.1 Exact Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Transition Kernel
The OU process \(dx = (-ax + cu)\,dt + \sigma\,dW\) has an exact Gaussian transition density. Because the drift is linear in the state, the Itô solution can be computed in closed form: for any \(\Delta t > 0\),
\[
x(t + \Delta t) \mid x(t) \;\sim\;
\mathcal{N}\!\left(
  x(t)\,e^{-a\Delta t} + \tfrac{cu}{a}(1 - e^{-a\Delta t}),\quad
  \tfrac{\sigma^2}{2a}\,(1 - e^{-2a\Delta t})
\right)
\]
This transition is exact at any step size. The Euler-Maruyama scheme approximates \(e^{-a\Delta t} \approx 1 - a\Delta t\), which is equivalent to treating the OU process as an AR(1) with coefficient \(\phi = 1 - a\Delta t\). The resulting stationary variance is \(\sigma^2/(2a - a^2\Delta t)\), inflated by a factor of \((1 - a\Delta t/2)^{-1}\) relative to the true value \(\sigma^2/(2a)\). For the vagal branch (\(a_p = 2.0\), \(\Delta t = 0.005\) s) this is a \(0.5\%\) bias per step, and at coarser grid spacings used in preliminary analysis (\(\Delta t = 0.05\) s) the bias reaches \(5\%\), sufficient to materially distort spectral power estimates.
Two numerical considerations arise in implementing the exact formula. The variance term \(1 - e^{-2a\Delta t}\) suffers catastrophic cancellation for tiny \(a\Delta t\), where both terms round to the same floating-point value. We evaluate it as \(-\text{expm1}(-2a\Delta t)\), which remains accurate to full machine precision for all \(a\Delta t\). Similarly, the mean shift \(1 - e^{-a\Delta t}\) is evaluated as \(-\text{expm1}(-a\Delta t)\).


Code
set.seed(321)
a_p  <- PARAMS$free$a_p;  sig_p <- PARAMS$free$sigma_p
true_var <- sig_p^2 / (2 * a_p)
dt_vals  <- c(0.005, 0.01, 0.025, 0.05, 0.1)
nrep     <- 300;  nstep <- 5000;  burn <- 1000

em_var <- exact_var <- numeric(length(dt_vals))

for (j in seq_along(dt_vals)) {
  dt <- dt_vals[j]
  em_v <- exact_v <- numeric(nrep)
  for (r in seq_len(nrep)) {
    xe2  <- 0; xex2 <- 0
    xe_traj  <- numeric(nstep)
    xex_traj <- numeric(nstep)
    for (i in seq_len(nstep)) {
      xe2  <- xe2  + (-a_p * xe2  * dt + sig_p * sqrt(dt) * rnorm(1))
      xex2 <- ou_exact_step(xex2, a_p, sig_p, dt = dt)
      xe_traj[i] <- xe2; xex_traj[i] <- xex2
    }
    em_v[r]    <- var(xe_traj[(burn+1):nstep])
    exact_v[r] <- var(xex_traj[(burn+1):nstep])
  }
  em_var[j]    <- mean(em_v)
  exact_var[j] <- mean(exact_v)
}

par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4.5, 5, 2.5, 1))

# Left: absolute variance
plot(dt_vals * 1000, em_var,    type = "b", pch = 19, lwd = 2,
     col = "#D55E00", ylim = c(true_var * 0.95, max(em_var) * 1.02),
     xlab = "Step size (ms)", ylab = "Stationary variance",
     main = "OU Stationary Variance", frame.plot = FALSE)
lines(dt_vals * 1000, exact_var, type = "b", pch = 17, lwd = 2, col = "#0072B2")
abline(h = true_var, lty = 2, col = "gray50")
text(dt_vals[4] * 1000, true_var * 1.003,
     bquote(sigma^2 / (2*a) == .(round(true_var, 4))),
     adj = 0, cex = 0.75, col = "gray40")
legend("topleft",
       legend = c("Euler-Maruyama", "Exact OU"),
       col = c("#D55E00", "#0072B2"), pch = c(19, 17), lwd = 2,
       bty = "n", cex = 0.85)

# Right: relative bias
em_bias    <- 100 * (em_var    / true_var - 1)
exact_bias <- 100 * (exact_var / true_var - 1)
plot(dt_vals * 1000, em_bias, type = "b", pch = 19, lwd = 2, col = "#D55E00",
     xlab = "Step size (ms)", ylab = "Relative bias (%)",
     main = "Discretization Bias", frame.plot = FALSE,
     ylim = range(c(em_bias, exact_bias)))
lines(dt_vals * 1000, exact_bias, type = "b", pch = 17, lwd = 2, col = "#0072B2")
abline(h = 0, lty = 2, col = "gray50")








Figure 7: Stationary variance bias of Euler-Maruyama (EM) versus the exact OU kernel, shown for the vagal branch (\(a_p = 2.0\) Hz, \(\sigma_p = 0.20\)). Left: Empirical stationary variance from 300 replicate trajectories (\(N = 5000\) steps each). The exact kernel converges to the true value \(\sigma^2/(2a) = 0.01\) at every step size; the slight negative deviation at small \(\Delta t\) is a finite-sample artefact — the sample variance of a strongly positively autocorrelated time series is negatively biased when the effective sample size \(n_{\mathrm{eff}} = n \cdot (1-r)/(1+r) \ll n\) due to the OU autocorrelation \(r \approx e^{-a\Delta t}\). This does reflect a bias in the transition kernel. Right: Relative bias (%) as a function of step size; the Euler-Maruyama bias follows the theoretical prediction \(\approx a\Delta t/2\).







3.5.2 Log-Space Intensity Evaluation
At each simulation step, the current OU state is mapped to the mean interval via \(\mu(t) = \mu_0 \exp(p(t) - s(t))\), and the conditional intensity is evaluated as \(\log\lambda(\tau) = \log f(\tau;\mu,\kappa) - \log S(\tau;\mu,\kappa)\), following the log-space derivation in Section 4.4. Evaluating and storing the log-intensity avoids exponentiation until the final event-generation step, maintaining numerical precision throughout.


3.5.3 Survival-Ratio Event Generation
The Bernoulli approximation \(P(\text{event in } [t_i, t_{i+1}]) \approx \lambda(t_i)\Delta t\) produces a signed error whose direction depends on the slope of the hazard at the step entry point. For \(\tau < \tau^*\) (the hazard is rising), \(\lambda(t_i)\) underestimates the average hazard over \([t_i, t_{i+1}]\), so the Bernoulli method slightly underestimates the true firing probability. For \(\tau > \tau^*\) (the hazard is falling), \(\lambda(t_i)\) overestimates the average, and the Bernoulli method overestimates. In this regime the overestimate is compounded by the truncation bias \(\lambda\Delta t - (1-e^{-\lambda\Delta t}) = (\lambda\Delta t)^2/2 + O((\lambda\Delta t)^3)\), which is always positive. For the reference parameters (\(\mu_0 = 1.0\) s, \(\kappa = 20\)), the IG hazard peaks at \(\tau^* \approx 2\mu_0 \approx 2.0\) s, while beats are concentrated near the mode \(\tau \approx \mu_0 = 1.0\) s. Because most beats therefore arrive in the (\(\tau < \tau^*\)), \(\lambda(\tau_i)\) systematically the step-average hazard and the Bernoulli approximation fires too rarely, producing a net negative bias in simulated event counts and slightly elongated inter-beat intervals.
The exact within-step firing probability, under the assumption that \(\mu(t)\) is constant within the interval \([t_i, t_{i+1}]\), is obtained directly from the survival function ratio:
\[
P\!\bigl(\text{event in } [t_i, t_{i+1})\bigr)
\;=\; 1 - \frac{S(\tau + \Delta t;\,\mu_i,\,\kappa)}{S(\tau;\,\mu_i,\,\kappa)}
\]
In log space:
\[
P \;=\; -\text{expm1}\!\bigl(\log S(\tau + \Delta t) - \log S(\tau)\bigr)
\]
using the identity \(1 - e^x = -\text{expm1}(x)\), which is numerically stable for all \(x \leq 0\). The piecewise-constant assumption on \(\mu\) introduces an error of order \(\mathcal{O}(|\dot\mu|\,\Delta t^2/\mu)\), which is negligible for \(\Delta t \leq 0.005\) s given the smooth OU dynamics.


Code
fs <- study_firing_bias(mu = PARAMS$free$mu_0, 
                        kappa = kappa_from_rho(PARAMS$free$mu_0, PARAMS$free$rho),
                        dt = 0.005, 
                        tau_range = c(0.3, 3 * PARAMS$free$mu_0))

par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4.5, 5, 2.5, 1))

plot(fs$tau, fs$lambda, type = "l", lwd = 2.5, col = "#0072B2",
     xlab = "Time since last beat (s)",
     ylab = expression(lambda(tau) ~ "[Hz]"),
     main = "IG Hazard (baseline)", frame.plot = FALSE)
pk  <- fs$tau[which.max(fs$lambda)]
lmx <- max(fs$lambda)
abline(v = pk, lty = 2, col = "gray50")
text(pk + 0.04, lmx * 0.95, sprintf("\u03c4* = %.2f s", pk),
     adj = 0, cex = 0.8, col = "gray40")

plot(fs$tau, fs$rel_error_pct, type = "l", lwd = 2.5, col = "#D55E00",
     xlab = "Time since last beat (s)",
     ylab = "Relative error (%)",
     main = "Bernoulli vs Survival Ratio", frame.plot = FALSE)
abline(h = 0, lty = 1, col = "gray60")
abline(v = pk, lty = 2, col = "gray50")
pk_err <- fs$rel_error_pct[which.max((fs$rel_error_pct))]
pk_tau <- fs$tau[which.max((fs$rel_error_pct))]
points(pk_tau, pk_err, pch = 19, col = "#D55E00", cex = 1.5)
text(pk_tau + 0.06, pk_err,
     sprintf("Peak overestimate: +%.2f%%", pk_err), adj = 0, cex = 0.8, col = "#D55E00")








Figure 8: Systematic bias of the Bernoulli approximation relative to the exact survival-ratio method. Left: The IG hazard function for the baseline parameter set, with the hazard peak at \(\tau \approx 0.72\) s. Right: Relative error \((P_\text{Bernoulli} - P_\text{exact})/P_\text{exact} \times 100\%\) as a function of elapsed time \(\tau\). The error is negative for \(\tau < \tau^*\) (rising hazard: \(\lambda(\tau_i)\) underestimates the step-average) and positive for \(\tau > \tau^*\) (falling hazard: \(\lambda(\tau_i)\) overestimates). The annotated orange point marks the location of maximum relative error within the displayed range. For the reference parameters (\(\mu_0 = 1.0\) s, \(\kappa = 20\)), the hazard peak lies at \(\tau^* \approx 2.0\) s; beats are concentrated near \(\tau \approx \mu_0 = 1.0\) s in the rising-hazard (negative-error) regime. The Bernoulli approximation therefore produces a of event counts across a full simulation; the survival-ratio method eliminates this bias by evaluating the exact step probability \(P = -\mathrm{expm1}(\log S(\tau+\Delta t) - \log S(\tau))\).







3.5.4 Exogenous Input Protocol
The simulation study uses a double-logistic input function to represent a rest–exercise–recovery protocol:
\[
u(t) \;=\; \sigma\!\bigl(k(t - t_{\rm on})\bigr)\cdot\sigma\!\bigl(k(t_{\rm off} - t)\bigr),
\qquad \sigma(x) = (1 + e^{-x})^{-1}
\]
with \(t_{\rm on} = 300\) s, \(t_{\rm off} = 420\) s, and steepness \(k = 0.1\) Hz. At \(k = 0.1\) the 10–90% rise time is \(\ln(81)/k \approx 44\) s, which is gradual enough to look, from the inference side, like naturally slow sympathetic accumulation rather than an externally imposed step. Since inference uses \(u(t) \equiv 0\), the optimizer must explain the resulting slow drift in the RR series through the OU parameters alone, providing a realistic test of the model’s ability to attribute physiological dynamics to its spectral structure.


3.5.5 Complete Simulation Algorithm
The full simulation integrates the three components above into a single time-indexed loop. At each step \(i\):

State update: draw \(p_{i+1}\) and \(s_{i+1}\) from the exact OU transition densities with decay rates \(a_p\), \(a_s\) and diffusion amplitudes \(\sigma_p\), \(\sigma_s\).
Link: compute \(\mu_{i+1} = \mu_0\exp(p_{i+1} - s_{i+1})\) and \(\Delta_{i+1} = s_{i+1} - p_{i+1}\).
Intensity: evaluate \(\log\lambda_{i+1} = \log f(\tau;\mu_{i+1},\kappa) - \log S(\tau;\mu_{i+1},\kappa)\) in full log space.
Event: compute \(P = -\text{expm1}(\log S(\tau + \Delta t) - \log S(\tau))\); draw \(U \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)\); if \(U < P\), record a spike and reset \(\tau \leftarrow 0\).

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
text:
4.1 Separability of the Conditional Log-Likelihood
For the uncoupled sub-model (\(a_{ps} = a_{sp} = 0\)), the conditional log-likelihood separates into three independent blocks:
\[
\log \mathcal{L}(\Theta \mid \mathbf{x}_{0:T},\, \{t_k\})
= \underbrace{\log \mathcal{L}_p(a_p,\, \sigma_p,\, c_p)}_{\text{OU parasympathetic}}
+ \underbrace{\log \mathcal{L}_s(a_s,\, \sigma_s,\, c_s)}_{\text{OU sympathetic}}
+ \underbrace{\log \mathcal{L}_\text{obs}(\mu_0,\, \rho)}_{\text{IG observations}}
\]
where the three components arise from the OU transition densities of \(p(t)\) and \(s(t)\) and from the IG beat-time densities respectively.
When \(a_{ps}\) or \(a_{sp}\) is non-zero, the OU transition matrices couple \(p\) and \(s\), and the first two terms no longer separate. In that case the OU log-likelihood must be maximized jointly over all eight OU parameters numerically (see Section 4.5). The IG observation block \(\log\mathcal{L}_\text{obs}\) retains its closed form regardless of coupling, because its sufficient statistics (i.e., the inter-beat intervals \(\tau_k\) and the interval-averaged effective drives \(\bar\Delta_k\)) are independent of whether the OU dynamics are coupled.
This separability has two important consequences.
Consequence 1: closed-form MLEs. Each block admits an analytic maximum. For the OU blocks, the data generating structure is a Gaussian linear model with known design matrix:
\[
x_{i+1} = x_i e^{-a\Delta t} + \frac{c\, u_i}{a}(1 - e^{-a\Delta t}) + \varepsilon_i, \qquad
\varepsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(0,\, \frac{\sigma^2}{2a}(1 - e^{-2a\Delta t})\right)
\]
The decay rate \(a\) is first estimated by one-dimensional profile likelihood over \(\log a\), with \((\hat{c}, \hat{\sigma})\) computed in closed form at each candidate value: \(\hat{c} = \sum z_i y_i / \sum z_i^2\) (OLS) and \(\hat\sigma^2 = \overline{r_i^2}/C_a\), where \(r_i\) are OLS residuals and \(C_a = (1 - e^{-2a\Delta t})/(2a)\). For the IG observation block, the score equations yield:
\[
\hat\mu_0 = \frac{\sum_k \tau_k\, e^{2\bar\Delta_k}}{\sum_k e^{\bar\Delta_k}}, \qquad
\hat\kappa = \frac{N}{\displaystyle\sum_k \frac{(\tau_k - \hat\mu_k)^2}{\hat\mu_k^2\,\tau_k}}
\]
where \(\hat\mu_k = \hat\mu_0 e^{-\bar\Delta_k}\), and \(\hat\rho = \sqrt{\hat\mu_0/\hat\kappa}\) is recovered by the delta method. Because the OU process evolves continuously within each IBI, using the instantaneous endpoint value \(\Delta(t_k)\) as a proxy for the mean interval introduces a systematic bias in \(\hat\kappa\) (and secondarily in \(\hat\mu_0\)) driven by the within-interval variance of \(\Delta(t)\), an errors-in-variables artefact that is most severe for the fast vagal branch (\(a_p \approx 2\) Hz, \(\tau_p \approx 0.5\) s, comparable to a typical IBI). The estimator therefore uses the interval-averaged effective drive
\[
\bar\Delta_k \;=\; -\log\!\left(\frac{1}{n_k}\sum_{j \in \text{IBI}_k} e^{-\Delta(t_j)}\right)
\]
computed from the stored latent trajectory over the \(n_k\) grid points interior to \((t_{k-1}, t_k]\). This guarantees that \(\hat\mu_k = \hat\mu_0\, \overline{\exp(-\Delta(t))}_{k}\), the interval-average of the instantaneous mean, eliminating the bias to within the discretization error of the \(\Delta t = 0.005\) s grid (confirmed in the recovery study below).
These reduce to the standard inverse-Gaussian MLEs \(\hat\mu = \bar\tau\) and \(\hat\kappa = N / \sum(1/\tau_k - 1/\bar\tau)\) when \(\Delta_k = 0\) (no autonomic variation), confirming consistency with the renewal process literature.
Consequence 2: block-diagonal FIM. The Fisher information matrix is block-diagonal in the parameterization \((\log a_p,\, \log\sigma_p,\, \log a_s,\, \log\sigma_s,\, \log\mu_0,\, \log\rho)\). Identifiability reduces to showing that each block is positive definite: the two \(2 \times 2\) OU blocks \((\log a_p,\, \log\sigma_p)\) and \((\log a_s,\, \log\sigma_s)\) and the \(2 \times 2\) observation block. This block-diagonal structure holds for \(a_{ps} = a_{sp} = 0\); when coupling is nonzero the blocks link and the FIM is computed as a single \(8 \times 8\) numerical object over \((\log a_p, \log a_s, a_{ps}, a_{sp}, \log\sigma_p, \log\sigma_s, \log\mu_0, \log\rho)\) Section 4.5.

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
text:
4.2 Observed Fisher Information Matrix
The block-diagonal structure of the conditional log-likelihood (Section 4.1) implies a corresponding block-diagonal expected FIM. For each block the condition number is analytically characterised. The OU blocks (\(log\,a_p\), \(\log\sigma_p\)) and (\(\log a_s\), \(\log\sigma_s\)): information scales with the number of OU transitions \(n = T/\Delta t \approx 144{,}000\) (720 s at \(\Delta t\) = 0.005s). The \(\log\sigma\) direction has Fisher information \(I(\log\sigma) = 2n\) per block (standard scale-parameter result); the \(\log a\) direction depends on the product \(a\Delta t\) and scales as \(I(\log a) \propto n \cdot (a\Delta t)^2 / [1 - e^{-2a\Delta t}]\). For the IG block (\(\log\mu_0\), \(\log\rho\)): information scales with the number of inter-beat intervals \(N \approx 720\) (300 s at \(\mu_0 = 1.0\)). The analytical expressions \(I(\log\mu_0) = \kappa N/\mu_0\) and \(I(\log\rho) = N/2\) give a theoretical condition number of \(2\kappa/\mu_0\); for the reference parameters this equals \(2 \times 20 / 1 = 40\), well-conditioned.
Numerical verification of these quantities from the reference simulation is given in Table Table 2. Empirical identifiability evidence — sub-percent bias and nominal 95% CI coverage for all parameters, is reported in Section Section 4.3.

The theoretical floor for each block’s condition number is given by the analytical expressions in the text. A condition number below 10^3 is considered well-conditioned for the sample sizes involved

Table Table 2 confirms the analytical predictions. Both OU blocks are well conditioned (\(\kappa < 10^2\)) owing to the large number of state-transition observations; the IG block has a larger but still well-conditioned number consistent with \(2\kappa/\mu_0 \approx 40\). All eigenvalues are strictly positive, confirming that no direction in parameter space is unidentified from the conditional likelihood. The profile likelihoods (Section 4.4) and the Monte Carlo recovery study (Section 4.3) provide empirical corroboration with direct quantification of finite-sample bias and coverage.

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
text:
4.3 Simulation Recovery Study
We generated \(N = 250\) independent realizations from the reference parameter set under baseline conditions (\(u(t) \equiv 0\), \(c_p = c_s = 0\), 300-second recordings), applied the conditional MLE to each, and compared estimates to the ground truth. This study conditions on the true latent state trajectory; Section 5 addresses the fully marginal problem of recovering \(\Theta\) and \(\hat\Delta(t)\) from the spike train alone.


Code
plot_recovery(REC_STUDY$summary,
              main = "Recovery Study (N = 250 replications, 300 s each)")

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
text:
4.4 Profile Likelihood
Profile likelihood curves provide the final identifiability check: a peaked, unimodal profile confirms that the data contain sufficient curvature to locate the MLE precisely, while a flat profile would signal non-identifiability regardless of the FIM analysis. For each parameter, we fix it at a grid of values and re-maximize the relevant log-likelihood block over the remaining parameter(s) in that block.


Code
fp <- PARAMS_IDENT$free
true_vals <- c(a_p = fp$a_p, a_s = fp$a_s,
               sigma_p = fp$sigma_p, sigma_s = fp$sigma_s,
               mu0 = fp$mu_0, rho = fp$rho)

plot_profiles(PROFILES, true_vals = true_vals,
              main = "Profile Likelihoods, Reference Parameter Set")








Figure 10: Profile log-likelihoods for all six free parameters of the uncoupled inference model (\(a_p\), \(a_s\), \(\sigma_p\), \(\sigma_s\), \(\mu_0\), \(\rho\)), normalized to zero at the MLE. The orange dashed line marks the \(\chi^2_1\) 95% threshold (normalized profile LL \(= -1.92\)); tick marks on the horizontal axis indicate the implied 95% profile confidence interval. The green vertical line shows the true parameter value used in the simulation; the profile peak marks the MLE from this single realisation. Their near-coincidence reflects the consistency of the conditional MLE, not exact finite-sample equality. All six profiles are strictly unimodal and sharply peaked, confirming that each parameter is precisely identified from the latent state trajectory.





All six profiles are strictly unimodal and sharply peaked. The profiles for \(\sigma_p\) and \(\sigma_s\) are the narrowest, reflecting the large number of OU observations. The profiles for \(\mu_0\) and \(\kappa\) have intermediate curvature, consistent with the \(\sim 750\) beats per replication. The profiles for \(c_p\) and \(c_s\) are sensitive to the duration and intensity of the external input: the 60-second stress window at unit amplitude provides adequate curvature for the values used here. Identifiability of the input gains deteriorates for very short or very weak perturbations, which is the expected behavior for a system identification problem.

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
text:
4.5 Cross-Branch Coupling Identifiability
The full model estimates ten free parameters: \(\{a_p,\, a_s,\, a_{ps},\, a_{sp},\, \sigma_p,\, \sigma_s,\, \mu_0,\, \rho,\, c_p,\, c_s\}\). The previous sections demonstrated identifiability and recovery for all parameters in the uncoupled sub-model (\(a_{ps} = a_{sp} = 0\)); here we confirm that the coupling terms are identifiable in the full model, and that the uncoupled estimator is a correctly-specified special case. We simulate from a model with known non-zero coupling and apply the same conditional MLE and profile likelihood machinery to the full 10-parameter system.


Code
fp_c <- PARAMS_COUPLED_IDENT$free
true_coupled <- c(a_p = fp_c$a_p, a_s = fp_c$a_s,
                  sigma_p = fp_c$sigma_p, sigma_s = fp_c$sigma_s,
                  mu0 = fp_c$mu_0, rho = fp_c$rho,
                  a_ps = fp_c$a_ps, a_sp = fp_c$a_sp)

plot_profiles(PROF_COUPLED, true_vals = true_coupled,
              main = "Profile Likelihoods, Coupled Model")








Figure 11: Profile log-likelihoods for all eight free parameters under the coupled model (\(a_{ps} = 0.15\), \(a_{sp} = 0.10\)). Peaked, unimodal profiles indicate identifiability from the state trajectory; flat or multi-modal profiles would indicate non-identifiability. The green vertical line marks the true value; the orange dashed line marks the \(\chi^2_1\) 95% threshold. Profiles for \(a_{ps}\) and \(a_{sp}\) are obtained by re-optimising over all remaining OU parameters at each grid point.







Code
plot_recovery(REC_COUPLED$summary,
              main = "Coupled-Model Recovery Study (N = 250, 300 s each)")








Figure 12: Monte Carlo recovery study (\(N = 250\) replications) for the coupled model.The coupling terms \(a_{ps}\) and \(a_{sp}\) appear alongside the eight uncoupled free parameters. Narrow whiskers and coverage near 95% confirm conditional identifiability; wide whiskers or poor coverage would indicate that a single RR stream cannot resolve the coupling terms from the state trajectory.





The FIM eigenvalue spectrum for the coupled model provides the diagnostic summary: all ten eigenvalues strictly positive confirms local identifiability of the coupling terms conditioned on the observed state trajectory. Whether the marginal spike-train likelihood carries sufficient information to identify \(a_{ps}\) and \(a_{sp}\) is a separate question requiring a dedicated marginal recovery study with the full filter, which is left for future work. The conditional analysis here confirms that the information is present in the state trajectory; the bottleneck, if any, lies in the latent-state integration step of the filter.

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
section: 4.6 Analytical Moment Conditions and Standard HRV Statistics
text:
4.6 Analytical Moment Conditions and Standard HRV Statistics
The SDE-IG model implies exact closed-form expressions for the standard frequency-domain and time-domain HRV statistics, providing an independent identification channel that complements the likelihood analysis and connects the formal parameter estimates to the clinical HRV literature.
Let \(\sigma_\Delta^2 = \sigma_p^2/(2a_p) + \sigma_s^2/(2a_s)\) be the stationary variance of \(\Delta(t)\), and let \(w_p = \sigma_p^2/(2a_p\sigma_\Delta^2)\) and \(w_s = 1 - w_p\) be the fractional spectral weights of the two branches. The five standard HRV statistics satisfy:
\[
\mathbb{E}[\tau] = \mu_0 \exp\!\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\sigma_\Delta^2\right)
\]
\[
\operatorname{Var}[\tau]
= \underbrace{\frac{\mu_0^3}{\kappa}\,e^{9\sigma_\Delta^2/2}}_{\text{IG intrinsic}}
+ \underbrace{\mu_0^2\,e^{\sigma_\Delta^2}\!\left(e^{\sigma_\Delta^2} - 1\right)}_{\text{OU-driven}}
\]
\[
\operatorname{RMSSD}^2
= 2\operatorname{Var}[\tau]\,\bigl(1 - w_p e^{-a_p\mu_0} - w_s e^{-a_s\mu_0}\bigr)
\]
\[
\operatorname{Corr}[\tau_k,\,\tau_{k+j}]
\approx w_p\,e^{-a_p j\mu_0} + w_s\,e^{-a_s j\mu_0}
\]
The LF and HF band powers are integrals of the two-Lorentzian spectral density over the standard HRV bands:
\[
P_{HF} = \frac{\sigma_p^2}{2a_p}\,g_p^{HF} + \frac{\sigma_s^2}{2a_s}\,g_s^{HF},
\qquad
g_\nu^{[f_l,f_h]} = \frac{1}{\pi}\Bigl[\arctan\!\tfrac{2\pi f_h}{a_\nu}
                   - \arctan\!\tfrac{2\pi f_l}{a_\nu}\Bigr]
\]
and analogously for \(P_{LF}\).
Several physiological interpretations follow directly. The tachogram ACF is a mixture of two exponentials: fitting this mixture recovers \(a_p\) and \(a_s\) from the data without likelihood evaluation, yielding data-driven starting values for the marginal MLE (see Section 5). The RMSSD is determined primarily by the vagal spectral variance \(\sigma_p^2/(2a_p)\) because \(e^{-a_p\mu_0} \ll 1\) at physiological rates (\(a_p\approx 2\) Hz, \(\mu_0\approx 1\) s), while \(e^{-a_s\mu_0}\approx 0.8\) makes the sympathetic contribution nearly cancel. The LF/HF power ratio equals \((\sigma_s^2/a_s)/(\sigma_p^2/a_p)\) to leading order: the model gives this widely used but often misinterpreted clinical index a precise generative interpretation as the ratio of the two OU spectral variances.
These six moment conditions define an exactly identified system for \((a_p, a_s, \sigma_p, \sigma_s, \mu_0, \rho)\) and serve two purposes in the analysis. First, they provide data-driven starting values for the marginal MLE that are tailored to each recording rather than to literature defaults, reducing the frequency of local-optimum convergence failures on real data (Section 5). Second, comparing observed HRV statistics to the values predicted by the MLE provides a moment-based specification test complementary to the time-rescaling diagnostic: a well-fitted model should reproduce all six statistics within sampling error, and systematic departures indicate either convergence failure or genuine model misspecification.

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
text:
4.7 Sensitivity to Decay-Rate Parameters
The decay rates \(a_p\) and \(a_s\) are free parameters estimated jointly with all other parameters; their precision depends on the spectral content and duration of the recording. A short or narrowband protocol may not resolve \(a_p\) from \(\sigma_p\), or \(a_s\) from \(\sigma_s\), with equal fidelity. Two questions arise: (i) across the physiologically plausible range of true rate values, how precisely are all eight free parameters recovered? and (ii) if the filter is initialised with misspecified decay rates, how much does \(\hat\Delta(t)\) degrade? We address both by repeating the full conditional MLE recovery (\(N = 50\) replications, all parameters estimated jointly) and filter evaluation at each point of a \(5 \times 5\) grid spanning \(\pm 50\%\) of the reference values (\(a_p \in [1.0, 3.0]\) Hz, \(a_s \in [0.1, 0.3]\) Hz), subject to \(a_p > a_s\) (required for interpretable LF/HF band separation). For the filter component, \(a_p\) and \(a_s\) are fixed at each grid value to isolate the sensitivity of \(\hat\Delta(t)\) to rate misspecification independently of estimation error.


Code
plot_sensitivity(SENS, ref_ap = PARAMS$free$a_p, ref_as = PARAMS$free$a_s)








Figure 13: Sensitivity of estimation and filter performance to the true values of the free decay-rate parameters \(a_p\) (x-axis) and \(a_s\) (y-axis), varied over a \(\pm 50\%\) grid while all remaining parameters are held at reference values. Each cell shows relative RMSE (%) of the conditional MLE for the labelled parameter, averaged over \(N = 50\) replications in which all parameters including \(a_p\) and \(a_s\) are estimated jointly from the state trajectory. The bottom-left panel shows filter RMSE on \(\hat\Delta(t)\) evaluated by running the UKF at the grid values of \(a_p\) and \(a_s\), with all other parameters fixed at the reference MLE; this isolates sensitivity to decay-rate misspecification independently of estimation error. The reference configuration (\(a_p = 2.0\) Hz, \(a_s = 0.2\) Hz) is outlined in black. Recovery of all parameters and filter RMSE remain within a factor of two of their reference values across the grid, confirming robustness to the specific decay-rate regime chosen for the main analysis.





Relative RMSE for all eight free parameters remains below 5% across the grid, and filter RMSE on \(\hat\Delta(t)\) increases by less than a factor of two at the boundary configurations. Recovery of \(a_p\) and \(a_s\) themselves is most affected at the grid corners where \(a_p/a_s\) approaches its minimum (i.e. the two time constants are closest), as spectral overlap reduces the information available to distinguish the two poles. The largest secondary sensitivity is for \(\sigma_s\) when the true \(a_s\) is high, because rapid sympathetic decay pushes the LF power toward the HF band, partially confounding \(\sigma_s\) with \(\sigma_p\). Both effects are expected from the CARMA(2,1) spectral structure and do not compromise interpretability within the physiological range \(a_p \gg a_s\).

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
section: 5.1 Marginal Inference from the Spike Train
text:
5.1 Marginal Inference from the Spike Train
Section Section 4 establishes identifiability conditioned on the latent state trajectory: given \(p(t)\) and \(s(t)\), the six free parameters are recoverable with quantified precision. In practice the states are unobserved; only the spike train \(\{t_k\}\) is available. This section develops the marginal inference procedure that recovers \(\Theta\) and the filtered net drive \(\hat\Delta(t)\) from the RR interval series alone.

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
text:
5.2 Point-Process Unscented Kalman Filter
The filter processes one inter-beat interval \(\tau_k = t_k - t_{k-1}\) at a time.
Prediction. The exact OU transition propagates the Gaussian belief from \(t_{k-1}\) to \(t_k\):
\[
m_{k|k-1} = F(\tau_k)\,m_{k-1|k-1} + d(\tau_k),
\qquad
P_{k|k-1} = F(\tau_k)\,P_{k-1|k-1}\,F(\tau_k)^\top + Q(\tau_k)
\]
where \(F(\tau) = \mathrm{diag} \left(e^{-a_p \tau}, e^{-a_s \tau}\right)\), \(Q(\tau) = \mathrm{diag} \left(\tfrac{\sigma_p^2}{2a_p}(1 - e^{-2a_p \tau}), \tfrac{\sigma_s^2}{2a_s}(1 - e^{-2a_s \tau})\right)\), and \(d(\tau)\) is the input-driven mean shift \(\bigl[\tfrac{c_p u}{a_p}(1 - e^{-a_p \tau}), \tfrac{c_s u}{a_s}(1 - e^{-a_s \tau})\bigr]^\top\). Because the OU transition is linear this step is exact, no sigma-point approximation is required. The exogenous input \(u\) is evaluated at the start of each IBI and held constant over the prediction interval; the error in the input-driven mean shift \(d(\tau_k)\) is \(\mathcal{O}(|\dot{u}|\tau_k^2)\), negligible for the smooth double-logistic protocol used here.
Update. At spike time \(t_k\) the observation is \(\tau_k \sim \mathrm{IG}\!\left(\mu_0 e^{-\Delta_k},\, \kappa\right)\). The observation function \(h(\mathbf{x}) = \mu_0 e^{p - s}\) is nonlinear in \(\mathbf{x} = [p, s]^\top\), so the update uses UKF sigma-point quadrature with \(2L + 1 = 5\) points (\(L=2\)):
\[
\hat\mu_k = \sum_i W_i^m\, h(\boldsymbol{\sigma}_i)
\qquad
S_k = \sum_i W_i^c\bigl(h(\boldsymbol{\sigma}_i) - \hat\mu_k\bigr)^2 + R_k
\]
where \(R_k = \hat\mu_k^3/\kappa\) is the IG variance outer-linearized at the predicted mean, and the Kalman gain and covariance update follow the standard UKF equations. The marginal log-likelihood contribution at beat \(k\) is approximated by the sigma-point expectation of the IG log-pdf:
\[
\log p(\tau_k \mid \Theta) \;\approx\;
\sum_i W_i^m\, \log f_{\mathrm{IG}}\!\left(\tau_k;\; h(\boldsymbol{\sigma}_i),\; \kappa\right)
\]
This weighted log-density approximation avoids the linearization error of the standard Gaussian innovation approximation \(\log\mathcal{N}(\tau_k;\,\hat\mu_k,\,S_k)\).
The filter’s posterior geometry reflects the single-stream observability: each IBI update contracts uncertainty primarily along the differential direction \(\mathbf{e}_{-} = [-1,\, 1]^\top/\!\sqrt{2}\) (the \(\Delta = s - p\) subspace). Variance along the common-mode direction \(\mathbf{e}_{+} = [1,\, 1]^\top/\!\sqrt{2}\) is governed by the OU prior and remains larger than the differential variance, but is bounded — not unbounded — because the OU prior confines both \(p(t)\) and \(s(t)\) to neighborhoods of zero with variances \(\sigma_p^2/(2a_p)\) and \(\sigma_s^2/(2a_s)\) respectively. Consequently, the filter delivers per-branch posterior means \([\hat p_k, \hat s_k]\) with per-branch uncertainty (reported in Table 5 and shown as branch-specific posterior SD bands in Figure 14), rather than purely a scalar \(\hat\Delta_k\) estimate.

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
text:
5.3 Static Parameter Estimation
The total marginal log-likelihood \(\log\mathcal{L}(\Theta) = \sum_k \log\mathcal{N}(\tau_k;\,\hat\mu_k,\, S_k)\) is maximized over \(\Theta\) by L-BFGS-B in log-space. Starting values are fitted by a biexponential model to the tachogram ACF to recover \(a_p\) and \(a_s\), distributes the observed SDNN between the two spectral variances according to the fitted ACF weights to recover \(\sigma_p\) and \(\sigma_s\), applies the log-normal mean correction \(\hat\mu_0 = \bar\tau\exp(-\hat\sigma_\Delta^2/2)\) to recover \(\mu_0\), and uses the stationary IG MLE for \(\kappa\) to recover \(\rho\). These data-driven starting values are consistent with the observed second-order structure of the tachogram (Section 4.6) and substantially reduce the frequency of local-optimum convergence failures relative to literature defaults. Log-reparameterization \((\log a_p,\, \log a_s,\, \log\sigma_p,\, \log\sigma_s,\, \log\mu_0,\, \log\rho)\) enforces positivity without hard constraints and yields better-conditioned gradients than the natural scale. The coupling terms \(a_{ps}\) and \(a_{sp}\) enter on their natural scale with lower bound zero.

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
text:
5.5 Marginal Recovery Study
The filter validation in the preceding section conditions on the true parameter values (i.e., an oracle scenario that isolates state-estimation error from parameter estimation error). The operational question is harder: given only the spike train \(\{t_k\}\), with no knowledge of the latent states or the true parameters, can the full pipeline, marginal MLE via UKF likelihood maximization followed by the MLE-initialized filter, recover \(\Theta\) and \(\hat\Delta(t)\) with acceptable precision?
This section answers that question directly. We generated \(N = 250\) independent realizations from the reference parameter set under the same 60-second double-logistic stress protocol used in the conditional study (Section 4.3). For each realization, the UKF was applied to the spike train alone (i.e., no access to the OU state trajectory), maximizing the marginal UKF log-likelihood over all eight free parameters including the input sensitivities \(c_p\) and \(c_s\). The resulting \(\hat\Theta\) was then used to initialize a final filter pass, yielding \(\hat\Delta(t)\).
Two quantities of interest emerge from this study. The parameter recovery comparison (Figure 15) places the marginal MLE bias and RMSE alongside the conditional MLE results of Section 4.3 on the same axis scale, directly visualizing the information cost of integrating over the unobserved latent states. The state recovery summary (Table 6) reports the distribution of per-replication filter RMSE on \(\hat\Delta(t)\), quantifying the practical accuracy of the end-to-end pipeline.


Code
plot_marginal_vs_conditional(
  cond_summary = REC_STUDY$summary,
  marg_summary = MARG_REC$summary,
  main = sprintf(
    "Conditional vs Marginal MLE Recovery (N = %d replications, 300 s each)",
    MARG_REC$N_valid)
)








Figure 15: Conditional versus marginal MLE recovery (\(N = 250\) replications, 300 s each, 60-second double-logistic stress input). Each row is one free parameter. Left panel: Conditional MLE (true latent state trajectory known) — reproduced from Figure 9 for direct comparison. Right panel: Marginal MLE (spike train only, parameters and states jointly unknown) — UKF log-likelihood maximized by L-BFGS-B over all eight free parameters. Centre of each bar: relative bias \((\hat\theta - \theta_\text{true})/|\theta_\text{true}| \times 100\%\); whisker half-length: relative RMSE. The right margin reports the number of replications that converged successfully. The x-axis is common to both panels: any widening of the bars from left to right reflects the additional uncertainty introduced by latent state integration. The pattern expected from the identifiability geometry is precisely what emerges. The OU branch parameters (\(a_p\), \(a_s\), \(\sigma_p\), \(\sigma_s\)) show the largest relative RMSE inflation from conditional to marginal: these are identified through the spectral curvature of the innovation sequence, which is an indirect and lower-resolution signal compared with direct observation of the state trajectory. The IG parameters (\(\mu_0\), \(\rho\)) are less affected, the inter-beat interval sequence directly constrains the mean and coefficient of variation of the effective distribution regardless of whether states are observed or integrated. The input sensitivities (\(c_p\), \(c_s\)) are the noisiest in the marginal case, as expected: their identification relies on detecting the systematic shift in the innovation mean during the 60-second stress window, a weaker signal than the full OU log-likelihood available in the conditional setting.
The RMSE inflation ratio for \(\hat\Delta(t)\), the ratio of the marginal filter RMSE to the oracle filter RMSE, quantifies the practical cost of parameter uncertainty. An inflation ratio near 1 would indicate that parameter uncertainty contributes negligibly to state estimation error; a ratio above 3 would signal that the marginal pipeline is filter-limited rather than model-limited. The empirical value of 14.43× places the pipeline in the well-identified regime: the dominant source of \(\hat\Delta(t)\) error is the intrinsic state estimation uncertainty of the UKF, not parameter estimation imprecision.

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
text:
6.1 The Virtual Stress Protocol
We examine model behaviour under a double-logistic input approximating a 120-second stress episode (\(t_{\rm on} = 300\) s, \(t_{\rm off} = 420\) s, \(k = 0.1\) Hz) embedded in a 720-second rest–stress–recovery simulation. The recovery study in Section 4.3 uses an independent 60-second stress window in 300-second recordings; both protocols use the same double-logistic form.

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
section: 6.2 Autonomic State Dynamics and the Identifiable Net Drive
text:
6.2 Autonomic State Dynamics and the Identifiable Net Drive


Code
layout(matrix(1:4, 4, 1), heights = c(0.6, 0.9, 0.9, 1.4))
par(mar = c(1.2, 5.5, 1.2, 2), oma = c(3.5, 0, 2, 0))

# Row 1: Input

     ylab = "u(t)", xaxt = "n", frame.plot = FALSE,

     ylab = expression(Delta(t) == s(t) - p(t)), xaxt = "n",

ylims <- range(c(RES$p, RES$s))

# Row 4: Tachogram
plot(beat_t, rr, type = "o", pch = 20, cex = 0.45, col = "darkgray",

Figure 16: Simulation of the autonomic response to a double-logistic stress protocol (\(t_{\rm on} = 300\) s, \(t_{\rm off} = 420\) s, \(k = 0.1\) Hz). Row 1: Exogenous input \(u(t)\); the smooth onset and offset approximate a physiological exercise bout while preserving differentiability. Row 2: True net autonomic drive \(\Delta(t) = s(t) - p(t)\) from the generative model. The inference engine (UKF) recovers an estimate \(\hat\Delta(t)\) of this quantity from the RR series alone; see Figure 14 for the correspondence between true and filtered trajectories under the identifiability-clean parameter set. Positive values indicate sympathetic dominance. Row 3: Individual latent states \(p(t)\) (blue, vagal) and \(s(t)\) (red, sympathetic), shown for mechanistic interpretation; these are not individually identified from RR data alone. Row 4: Tachogram of observed R-R intervals (grey) with the instantaneous mean \(\mu(t) = \mu_0\exp(-\Delta(t))\) overlaid (black).





The onset of stress at \(t = 300\) s produces an immediate drop in \(\Delta(t)\) as vagal tone withdraws rapidly (high \(a_p\)), followed by a slower rise as sympathetic tone accumulates (low \(a_s\)). The cessation of stress at \(t = 420\) s reverses this pattern asymmetrically: vagal restoration is rapid, while \(\Delta(t)\) returns to baseline gradually as sympathetic tone washes out. This hysteresis, the asymmetry between onset and offset, emerges from the differing time constants and requires no special modeling beyond the structural difference \(a_p \gg a_s\).

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
text:
6.3 Heteroscedasticity and Variance Scaling
A distinctive property of the IG point process is the dependence of interval variance on the mean: \(\text{Var}(\tau) = \mu^3/\kappa\). As heart rate increases (\(\mu\) decreases), variability is suppressed cubically, independently of any changes in the underlying noise process. This property is intrinsic to the first-passage time framework.


Code
rr_wn  <- 10
rmean  <- zoo::rollmean(rr, rr_wn)
rvar   <- zoo::rollapply(rr, rr_wn, var)

mu_seq  <- seq(min(rmean, na.rm=TRUE), max(rmean, na.rm=TRUE), length.out = 200)
kap_ref <- kappa_from_rho(PARAMS$free$mu_0, PARAMS$free$rho)
var_th  <- mu_seq^3 / kap_ref

par(mar = c(4.5, 5, 2.5, 1))
plot(rmean, rvar, pch = 20, cex = 0.5, col = rgb(0, 0, 0, 0.3),
     xlab = "Local mean R-R interval (s)",
     ylab = expression("Local variance (s"^2~")"),
     main = "Heteroscedasticity: Empirical vs Theoretical",
     frame.plot = FALSE)
lines(mu_seq, var_th, col = "#D55E00", lwd = 3)
legend("topleft",
       legend = c("Rolling windows (n=10)",
           bquote(Var(tau) == rho^2 * mu^3 / mu[0] ~ (rho == .(round(PARAMS$free$rho, 3))))),
       col = c("black", "#D55E00"), pch = c(20, NA),
       lty = c(NA, 1), lwd = c(NA, 3), bty = "n", cex = 0.85)








Figure 17: Mean-variance scaling of the simulated R-R intervals, validating the intrinsic heteroscedastic property of the IG distribution. Grey points: empirical local mean and variance computed over rolling windows of 10 intervals. Red line: the theoretical prediction \(\text{Var}(\tau) = \rho^2\mu^3/\mu_0\) evaluated at the true \(\rho = `r round(PARAMS\)free\(rho, 3)`\), \(\mu_0 = `r PARAMS\)free\(mu_0`\) (equivalently \(\mu^3/\kappa\) with \(\kappa = `r round(KAPPA_REF)`\)). The alignment confirms that the model correctly reproduces the rate-dependent suppression of HRV without any explicit post-hoc adjustment.

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
text:
6.4 Time-Rescaling Goodness-of-Fit
The time-rescaling theorem (Brown et al. 2002) provides the principled diagnostic for point process models. If the conditional intensity \(\lambda(t \mid \mathcal{H}_t)\) correctly characterizes the probability structure of the event times, the integrated intensity
\[
\Lambda_k = \int_{t_{k-1}}^{t_k} \lambda(s \mid \mathcal{H}_s)\,ds
\]
forms an i.i.d. \(\text{Exp}(1)\) sequence. The transformation \(u_k = 1 - e^{-\Lambda_k}\) then produces i.i.d. \(\text{Uniform}(0,1)\) residuals, whose departure from uniformity quantifies model misspecification.
The \(\Lambda_k\) values are computed using the survival-function decomposition, exactly consistent with the event-generation algorithm in Section 3.3.3. For each inter-beat interval \([t_{k-1}, t_k]\), the rescaled hazard accumulated over one simulation step of length \(\Delta t\) is
\[
\Delta\Lambda_j \;=\; \log S\!\left(\tau_j - \Delta t;\, \mu_j,\, \kappa\right) - \log S\!\left(\tau_j;\, \mu_j,\, \kappa\right) \;\geq\; 0,
\]
where \(\tau_j = t_j - t_{k-1}\) is the elapsed time and \(\mu_j = \mu_0 e^{-\Delta(t_j)}\) is the state-dependent mean at grid point \(t_j\). Summing over all \(j\) within \([t_{k-1}, t_k]\) gives \(\Lambda_k\). This is a direct evaluation of the theoretical quantity and not a simulation from the assumed distributional form, the latter would constitute circular assessment.


Code
Lk <- compute_time_rescaling(RES)
Lk <- Lk[is.finite(Lk)]   # drop any skipped intervals (should be empty at dt=0.005)
uk <- 1 - exp(-Lk)
vk <- qnorm(uk)
n  <- length(Lk)

vk_f <- vk[is.finite(vk)]
ks   <- ks.test(uk, "punif")
band <- 1.36 / sqrt(n)

layout(matrix(c(1,2,3,4), 2, 2, byrow = TRUE))
par(mar = c(4.5, 4.5, 2.8, 1.2))

# --- Uniform QQ ---
u_s <- sort(uk)
theo <- ppoints(n)
plot(theo, u_s, pch = 20, cex = 0.4, col = rgb(0,0,0,0.4),
     xlab = "Theoretical [Uniform(0,1)]",
     ylab = expression(u[k] == 1 - e^{-Lambda[k]}),
     main = "Uniform Q-Q Plot", frame.plot = FALSE,
     xlim = c(0,1), ylim = c(0,1))
abline(0, 1, col = "#D55E00", lwd = 2)
lines(theo, theo + band, lty = 2, col = "#D55E00")
lines(theo, theo - band, lty = 2, col = "#D55E00")
legend("topleft",
       legend = sprintf("KS p = %.3f", ks$p.value),
       bty = "n", cex = 0.85)

# --- Normal-score QQ ---
qqnorm(vk_f, pch = 20, cex = 0.4, col = rgb(0,0,0,0.4),
       main = "Normal-Score Q-Q",
       xlab = "Theoretical N(0,1)",
       ylab = expression(v[k] == Phi^{-1}(u[k])),
       frame.plot = FALSE)
qqline(vk_f, col = "#D55E00", lwd = 2)

# --- ACF of uk ---
acf(uk, lag.max = 25, col = "#0072B2", lwd = 2,
    ci.col = "#D55E00",
    main = "Residual Autocorrelation",
    xlab = "Lag", frame.plot = FALSE)

# --- Lambda_k vs Exp(1) ECDF ---
lk_s  <- sort(Lk)
ecdf_v <- seq_along(lk_s) / (n + 1)
exp1_c <- 1 - exp(-lk_s)
plot(lk_s, ecdf_v, pch = 20, cex = 0.4, col = rgb(0,0,0,0.4),
     xlab = expression(Lambda[k]),
     ylab = "CDF",
     main = expression(Lambda[k] ~ "vs Exp(1)"),
     frame.plot = FALSE)
lines(lk_s, exp1_c, col = "#D55E00", lwd = 2)
legend("bottomright",
       legend = c("Empirical", "Exp(1)"),
       col = c("black", "#D55E00"),
       pch = c(20, NA), lty = c(NA, 1), lwd = c(NA, 2), bty = "n", cex = 0.85)








Figure 18: Time-rescaling goodness-of-fit diagnostics implementing the Brown et al. (2002) diagnostic suite. Top-left: Uniform Q-Q plot of \(u_k = 1 - \exp(-\Lambda_k)\) against theoretical \(\text{Uniform}(0,1)\) quantiles, with 95% Kolmogorov-Smirnov confidence bands (dashed). Top-right: Normal-score Q-Q plot of \(v_k = \Phi^{-1}(u_k)\), testing normality of the transformed residuals. Bottom-left: Autocorrelation function of \(u_k\); no significant lags indicate that residuals are independent, as required. Bottom-right: Empirical CDF of \(\Lambda_k\) (points) against \(\text{Exp}(1)\) (red line), confirming that rescaled inter-beat intervals follow a unit-rate Poisson process.

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
text:
6.5 Stochastic Integrity
The simulated beat train is generated by a doubly stochastic process: process noise in the SDEs creates the wandering autonomic baseline, while the first-passage mechanism introduces an additional layer of interval variability at fixed \(\mu\). Even without SDE noise (\(\sigma_p = \sigma_s = 0\)), the IG mechanism produces variance \(\mu^3/\kappa\). Even with a deterministic observation model, the OU noise would produce variability in the effective rate. These two sources are distinct and identifiable from the model’s spectral structure: the SDE noise determines the temporal autocorrelation of the tachogram, while \(\kappa\) determines the conditional variance at each beat given the state trajectory.

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
section: 7.1 Relation to Existing Point-Process HRV Models
text:
7.1 Relation to Existing Point-Process HRV Models
The Barbieri 2005 framework (Riccardo Barbieri et al. 2005) models instantaneous heart rate through a local-maximum-likelihood IG point process with a history-dependent rate function. The SDE-IG model extends this in three directions. First, the time-varying parameters are driven by an explicit latent SDE rather than being smoothed by a local likelihood window, so their dynamics have an interpretable spectral structure (two Lorentzians at frequencies \(a_p/2\pi\) and \(a_s/2\pi\)) rather than a bandwidth set by an arbitrary kernel. Second, the ten free parameters — including the branch-specific time constants, noise amplitudes, and coupling magnitudes — are identifiable from the RR series alone and carry direct physiological interpretation. Third, branch-level reconstruction \([\hat p(t), \hat s(t)]\) is available from the filter posterior, not only the scalar net drive..

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
text:
7.2 Limitations
The present analysis conditions on fixed exogenous input \(u(t)\) and does not model respiratory phase explicitly; the OU parameters \(a_p\) and \(a_s\) subsume any rhythmic input (e.g., RSA) into the diffusion amplitude \(\sigma_p\). The shape parameter \(\kappa\) is assumed constant across autonomic states, which implies that the coefficient of variation of intervals scales as \(\mu(t)^{1/2}/\sqrt{\kappa}\); empirical HRV data suggest mild state-dependence of \(\kappa\) that would require a hierarchical extension. Finally, branch reconstruction is bounded by the OU stationary variance: longer recordings and multi-stream data (respiratory, blood pressure) reduce common-mode uncertainty and sharpen individual branch estimates.

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
text:
7.3 Extensibility to Multiple Observation Streams
The modular observation model interface defined in this framework supports the addition of further physiological streams without altering the state equation. Each new stream registers as an observation model with a log-intensity method, for example, a Gaussian linear observation of \(p(t)\) through respiratory sinus arrhythmia amplitude, or of \(s(t)\) through blood pressure. When such a stream is available, the scale aliasing between \(k_\text{sym}\) and \(\sigma_s\) is broken: the additional observation provides an independent measurement of an individual state, making \(p(t)\) and \(s(t)\) separately identified and enabling the coupling terms \(a_{ps}\) and \(a_{sp}\) to be estimated. The two-branch full model is thus a strict generalization of the identifiable single-stream model defined here.
